data.title: U.S. Bancorp Q1 Earnings: Growth Without The Fireworks
data.description:
Introduction

The super-regional bank U.S. Bancorp (USB) released its first-quarter 2026 earnings yesterday amid a broad relief rally fueled by negotiations over a deal between the U.S. and Iran and the associated optimism. However, USB stock edged lower yesterday, suggesting that the results were somewhat disappointing.

U.S. Bancorp is one of the few bank stocks I own, and I have written about it more or less regularly here on Seeking Alpha. In my last article, I discussed the bank's third-quarter results, which confirmed my generally constructive attitude. All in all, I found that the valuation of USB stock largely reflected the fundamentals, which is why I downgraded it from my previous “buy” rating in mid-April 2025 to “hold.”

In this update, I will share my assessment of U.S. Bancorp's Q1 2026 growth, profitability, and, of course, credit quality—also against the backdrop of the domestic average and the continued steepening of the interest rates. Finally, I will provide a brief valuation update, highlighting why I continue to rate USB stock as “hold.”

U.S. Bancorp Q1 2026 Earnings: Steady As She Goes

First quarter earnings per share (EPS) came in at $1.18, roughly 4% ahead of consensus and up 15% year-over-year. As is mostly the case with U.S. Bancorp, there were no earnings adjustments. Net revenues were $7.3 billion for the quarter, an increase of roughly 5% year-over-year. I was encouraged to see that U.S. Bancorp once again posted healthy positive operating leverage of more than 4 percentage points.

Average total loans grew by a reassuring 2.4% compared to the prior quarter (Figure 1), whereas year-over-year, the bank delivered loan growth of 3.8%. On the earnings call, CFO John Stern expressed confidence that loan growth for the full year will likely be even stronger. Corporate banking is expected to remain the main growth driver—in this segment, USB reported year-over-year growth of 11.4%. Mortgage loans to retail customers declined by 2% compared to the previous year's first quarter, which I believe is understandable given the current environment.

Net interest income grew by 4.1% year-over-year, largely thanks to the aforementioned strong growth in commercial lending but also due to the repricing of fixed assets. The latter helped the year-over-year comparison of net interest margin, which came in at 2.77%. So even though USB's net interest margin has improved by 5 basis points, I would argue that it could be even higher. The yield curve—illustrated by daily Treasury yields in Figure 2—continues to normalize, thereby strengthening banks' ability to make an increasingly significant contribution to interest income through duration management. Of course, this applies only on the condition that credit quality is maintained, especially for long-term loans.

Regarding noninterest income, I was encouraged to see fee-based revenues up 6.9% year-over-year. I compiled an overview of the individual contributors to noninterest income in Figure 3, which confirms U.S. Bancorp's continued broad diversification and good growth in the largest domains.

Importantly, fee-based income represented more than 40% of total quarterly net revenues, and there was—once again—almost no impact from securities-related gains or losses. This confirms that a significant portion of U.S. Bancorp's revenue is not directly tied to interest rates. Given the stickiness of fee-based revenues in various segments, I would also argue that U.S. Bancorp is less sensitive to economic cycles than competitors that focus on traditional banking.

During the earnings call, management confirmed its full-year net revenue guidance in the range of 4% to 6%, indicating that fee-based revenues will also continue to grow in the mid-single-digit range.

On the liabilities side of the balance sheet, deposits remained essentially unchanged compared to the prior quarter (Figure 4) but rose by 1.7% year-over-year. Growth was primarily seen in interest-bearing deposits and across most segments. While this is certainly no cause for excitement, it is entirely appropriate and manageable given the ongoing significant loan growth on the assets side of the balance sheet.

U.S. Bancorp's efficiency ratio improved by a solid 2.6% year-over-year but rose by 80 basis points compared to the previous quarter. From a longer-term perspective (Figure 5), I believe an efficiency ratio below 60% is solid in the case at hand. However, since U.S. Bancorp has historically been among the leaner banks in the U.S., I would like to see the efficiency ratio continue to decline going forward. Given various inflationary factors, this is, of course, not easy, but the use of AI is likely to represent a net tailwind for U.S. Bancorp and the sector as a whole. At the same time, AI is certainly a longer-term risk that should be considered in connection with (partial) disruptions at banks and payment service providers.

Return on tangible common equity (ROTCE) declined by 50 basis points year-over-year and 140 basis points quarter-over-quarter but remained at a very solid level of 17.0% in the first quarter of 2026. By comparison, Truist Financial Corporation (TFC), a comparable but more traditionally oriented peer of U.S. Bancorp, recorded an ROTCE of 12.7% in 2025 (results for the first quarter of 2026 will be released today).

From the perspective of asset quality, the bank remains on solid footing, in my view. Net charge-offs were up modestly compared to the prior quarter (Figure 6) but remained unproblematic and close to the domestic average (Figure 6). Allowances for credit losses increased by $50 million, or less than 1% of the prior quarter's allowance, largely due to loan portfolio growth. Allowances in the context of commercial real estate (CRE) loans actually declined in the last two quarters, after two quarters of noticeable increases.

Looking at non-performing loans (NPL), about one-third of them are associated with commercial real estate. While this may sound problematic, the number should be viewed in the context of U.S. Bancorp's undoubtedly solid NPL ratio. Non-performing loans, expressed as a percentage of total loans and other real estate, have declined for six consecutive quarters and have declined to less than 0.4% in Q1 2026 (Figure 7).

Finally, the bank continues to maintain a solid capitalization, as evidenced by its Common Equity Tier 1 (CET1) ratio of 10.8%—unchanged from both the previous year's first quarter and the prior quarter, despite a moderate dividend hike in September and the continuation of share repurchases. In the first quarter of 2026, U.S. Bancorp repurchased approximately 5 million shares. Unlike in previous quarters, however, the repurchases were only sufficient to offset shares issued for incentive programs and other purposes, even though the volume of repurchased shares increased from $100 million to $200 million. On the earnings call, CFO Stern hinted at slightly increasing buybacks over the course of the year.

In general, U.S. Bancorp is among the banks that take a rather cautious stance toward buybacks. While a total of approximately $9 billion in dividends on common stock has been paid out over the past three years, buybacks amounted to only about $700 million.

It could be argued that U.S. Bancorp's growth, profitability, and capitalization would justify somewhat more aggressive share buybacks. However, as a long-term shareholder, I have no objection to the relatively moderate pace considering long-term financial stability. Furthermore, since U.S. Bancorp stock looks fairly valued relative to both its book value and tangible book value (Figure 8), I would argue that the return on investment for shares repurchased at current levels is not particularly convincing. Although banks are not typically valued using earnings and revenue multiples, it is probably worth noting that USB stock is also fairly valued from this perspective.

Summary And Conclusion

U.S. Bancorp posted solid results for the first quarter of 2026, driven primarily by strong loan growth, particularly in the commercial segment. Noninterest income, which is largely fee-based, rose by a healthy 7% compared to the same period last year and continues to account for more than 40% of total net revenues.

The bank once again recorded significant positive operating leverage, so Q1 2026 marks the seventh consecutive quarter of progress in the right direction. From a six-year historical perspective, USB's efficiency ratio is near the lower end of the scale. Nevertheless, I would like to see a continued focus on lean operations—after all, U.S. Bancorp has historically been among the more efficient banks in the U.S. A lean cost structure should also help the bank maintain its profitability if net interest income eventually declines along with interest rates.

Asset quality also remained solid, as did capitalization. From this perspective, the cautious stance on share buybacks seems somewhat unjustified. However, since U.S. Bancorp stock appears fairly valued based on book value and historical earnings and revenue multiples, I don't mind the focus on dividends and a strong capitalization.

Personally, I wouldn't mind if my USB holdings were slightly larger in terms of invested capital. However, given the fair valuation and the fact that there are several more attractive investment opportunities at the moment, I see no reason to upgrade USB stock to “buy.”

Thank you very much for reading my latest article. Whether you agree or disagree with my conclusions, I always welcome your opinion and feedback in the comments below. And if there's anything I should improve or expand on in future articles, drop me a line as well. As always, please consider this article only as a first step in your own due diligence.

data.title: Chiron Real Estate: From High-Teens To Sub-10x FFO, A Rebound Case
data.description:
Introduction

As a (contrarian) REIT investor, I solely cover REITs on Seeking Alpha. Preferably REITs that are disliked by the market and are undervalued beyond what I deem to be reasonable.

Today I want to go over one of my more recent stock purchases: Chiron Real Estate (XRN). Together with EPR Properties (EPR), Realty Income (O) and Modiv Industrial (MDV), these are now my monthly paying dividend REITs.

Today I will provide you with the core reasons why I decided to open a position in this company. One of which I already went over: the fact that XRN pays a monthly dividend. A monthly dividend provides me with more liquidity that really helps me since my wife and I don't really save a lot of money. We are still relatively young (32 and 29), so the bulk of our income is derived from good old labor. We don't have any kids (yet), and we cover our living expenses by a wide margin. A monthly dividend provides us with more flexibility to direct money where it's most needed on a month-to-month basis: a holiday, new expensive clothing, or a larger amount of money to invest into the stock market. We both have ADHD, so we really like to decide ad hoc. Since we have the financial flexibility to live our lives this way, why not?

Investing is a very personal journey, so the coming chapters will solely cover the "facts" of Chiron Real Estate. Some inside information about my personal life can provide some color on the reasons behind me and my wife's investment decision. I'm providing my fellow investors with some background information that might show you why I make certain decisions.

A lot of dividend investors are not like me. They are a (little) bit older, more risk-averse (for that reason and others), and, of course, flooded with way more life experiences than me. I'll make sure to provide all of you with a thorough analysis of this company, after which you can decide for yourself whether to invest alongside me, or skip this ticker entirely.

Let us begin!

Risk assessment

No one knows at what price a stock will be trading tomorrow or even next quarter. All we can do is analyze a company and see whether there are fundamental reasons to buy a stock (or skip on it).

Regarding REITs, the dividend (safety) and the margin of safety are important topics to decide whether to buy into a REIT or not. In order to assess these topics, we have to start with some sort of risk assessment. I'll start by going over the portfolio in order to see if we can spot some sort of overexposure in this regard.

Portfolio diversification

Chiron Real Estate focuses on investing in and managing critical healthcare properties: 72% Outpatient Medical, 16% Inpatient Rehab and 12% in "other" assets. Around 7% of the average base rent [ABR] is coming from the largest tenant (Lifepoint Health), and the top 10 tenants are responsible for 38.8% of ABR. To me this looks healthy and manageable, but if XRN could decrease its exposure to Lifepoint Health, that surely would be a good thing. 17% of ABR is derived from Texas. Since exposure to one single region is less of a deal compared to tenant exposure, I don't see any meaningful risk from a diversification perspective.

Refinancing risk

XRN has been busy 'cleaning up' the balance sheet, with no upcoming refinancing in 2026 and 2027. This provides the company with time to use retained earnings (that will grow because of rent escalators) to further deleverage the company. If rates go down in the coming years, the stock only has one way to go... up!

The stock is too cheap

REIT investors want a sustainable and, preferably, growing dividend. When you buy Chiron's stock today, you get a monthly dividend of $0.25. A 8.5% dividend yield at today's price ($35.24). Since XRN is currently focused on stabilizing its balance sheet (Feb. 2026 investor presentation, page 8), I play it safe and only expect total returns coming from the current dividend yield combined with another 2.1% coming from rent escalators (page 10). You arrive at an expected - conservative - total return of 10.6%.

REITs are leveraged vehicles, which means that they use leverage to boost earnings. When rates go up, REIT valuations go down due to opportunity costs (the 'risk free' 10-year treasury) and higher interest rates that lower [A]FFO due to higher interest expenses.

Both the tariffs and the war in Iran are pushing inflation higher, leading to lower REIT valuations because of the interest rate sensitivity (and rates go up to combat inflation). If the Democrats win the midterms, Trump's policies may become more moderate because a divided government limits his ability to push measures that some would view as being a bit aggressive. In my humble opinion, more measured policies will benefit the economy (and push inflation lower).

If things normalize, inflation declines, and rate cuts follow, I expect all REITs to get back to a more reasonable valuation, Chiron Real Estate included.

But let's dive a bit deeper, shall we.

XRN shows us that the company traded at 19.5x FFO back in Dec. 2021 compared to 8.9x in Feb. 2026. Q4 2025 FFO came in at $0.97 ($3.88 annualized). A P/FFO multiple of 9x.

But 'Funds From Operations' is a surface-level profit metric for REITs, since it does not account for no-cash adjustments such as straight-line rent, recurring cash outflows like leasing costs and maintenance capex. So it's best to compare the free cash flow using the Q4 2021 and Q4 2025 data.

Back in 2021, XRN reported adjusted funds from operations (AFFO) to provide a metric better served for dividend coverage analysis compared to FFO. The FFO number includes straight-line rent: the average rent that a tenant pays during the entire lease. Due to rent escalators, straight-line rent overstates earnings in the first half of the lease term, as reported revenue exceeds actual cash rent. When I go over the Q4 2025 numbers, the company has shifted towards 'Funds available for distribution' [FAD]. FAD goes a step further by deducting recurring capital expenditures and leasing costs (like 'tenant improvements' and 'leasing commissions') from FFO, making it an even more conservative metric (compared to AFFO) to assess the true cash available for dividend payments.

To make a (more or less) fair comparison, I decided to subtract 25% of the full-year 2021 capital expenditures on existing real estate investments as reported in the Q4 2021 supplemental presentation (page 8). This Q4 2021 number came in at $2,351 (25%), lowering the AFFO from $16,380 to $14,028. We arrive at $0.205 in FAD per share. During Q3 2025, a one-for-five reverse split was done, so I had to multiply all the Q4 2021 numbers by 5. I took the liberty of including the leverage ratio as well, because the market valuation of a REIT includes - among others - the leverage ratio. There are more metrics to look at, but I have to pick and choose. As a percentage of FFO, the dividend coverage has decreased slightly during the years, but the FAD payout ratio became healthier. The leverage crept up a little, but since we are in a higher interest-rate environment, the current leverage definitely should put pressure on the valuation of the stock. And let's not forget that during 2021, the company was making acquisitions. Today, the company is busy deleveraging the balance sheet. A REIT in 'growth-modus' is worth more, as you might have guessed.

We could reason that if rates go down, Chiron Real Estate should be a real winner since the leverage is pretty high and any windfall coming from lower interest expenses would really help the stock. The REIT could get back into growth mode, boosting future FFO and therefore the valuation of the stock.

The lower FAD payout ratio provides some cushion in the current environment, but the current dividend might not be safe enough for more risk-averse investors. On the other hand, your starting yield is very high, so you get compensated for taking on this extra risk.

It made sense for XRN to trade at richer valuations back in 2021. Rates were lower, and the company was growing. But does it make sense for this REIT to go from 19.5x all the way down to around 9x? I don't think so. Especially when you take into consideration the fact that years of inflation have pushed construction costs up by a lot (43% in the past five years). This will benefit the portfolio value of REITs in general, XRN included. It will also make it harder to get a good enough economic return that justifies building new properties, restraining supply while demand is growing (because of the aging U.S. population). This long-term tailwind benefits some of my other holdings like Healthpeak Properties (DOC) and Medical Properties Trust (MPT) as well. Investors tend to focus on the short-term, but it is important to look (years) ahead to understand where the market will probably gravitate to.

Cap rate analysis

During Q4 2025, XRN sold two non-core assets for gross proceeds of $11.3 million, resulting in a $0.4 million loss (minus 3.5%). Is this a bad thing? Well, selling at a loss might be a bad sign, but only if you compare it to the current trading range of a company's (common) equity.

A cap rate analysis gives you a sense of the equity value of a REIT portfolio. I start by taking the annualized cash net operating income (NOI) of $119 million (page 2). When I take the 2022–2025 acquisition cap rate of 7.8% and apply it to XRN, we arrive at the following company portfolio valuation: $119 million NOI / 0.078 = $1.525 billion.

If someone wants to achieve a 7.8% unleveraged return on a property, $1.5 billion is what you have to pay to acquire XRN. In order to get us to the equity value of the common shares, we need to subtract the consolidated debt ($653.9 million) and we arrive at $868 million. We also have to subtract the liquidation value of the preferred stock ($129 million at the end of 2025). We add the 'cash and cash equivalents' on the balance sheet ($9 million) and arrive at $748 million.

When dividing the $748 million across the 14.516 million shares and units outstanding, the intrinsic value comes in at $51.5 per share.

XRN has sold some non-core assets at a slight loss to book value (minus 3.5%), but equity is trading at around 32% below its intrinsic value. To me, this doesn't make a lot of sense. A clear example of Mr. Market overreacting.

Conclusion

I rate Chiron Real Estate a 'buy.' My core reasons are the following:

  • A meaningful margin of safety given the current price compared to the intrinsic value per share
  • No refinancing risk in the coming two years
  • A double-digit yield to start with (dividend + growth)
  • A monthly dividend to boost liquidity (that can be used to buy more cheap shares at a faster pace)
  • Management alignment with shareholders*

*The fact that management bought a pretty decent amount of stock themselves during 2025, is another indicator that shares are undervalued at the current price.

There are always risks when taking a position in any REIT. Regarding Chiron Real Estate, the higher leverage gives you more exposure to where interest rates are going. XRN could be viewed as a 'bet on the Fed.' If rates go up in the coming years, refinancing will become more expensive, and the dividend might be on the chopping board. But if rates go down, the reward will be bigger as well. In the meantime, the high dividend yield compensates for this risk. Whether it is enough for you, depends.

For retired dividend investors that rely on a steady dividend paycheck to pay the bills, there are better and safer REITs to pick from. But you can decide to initiate a small position, use a DCA method, or go all-in. It is really up to you. I can sleep well by making this a 5% position, but it's really personal. I will just follow management and sit on my sizable position for years to come (Ceteris Paribus).

I started covering this stock today, and I understand that there is a lot more to talk about. This certainly won't be my last article on this specific ticker. Feel free to reach out to me in the comment section below. As always, I will be there.

Thanks for reading.

data.title: Salesforce: Why I Have Strong Conviction In This Stock
data.description:
Agentic AI Adoption Has Reached a Key Milestone

Even strong 4Q earnings couldn't save Salesforce, Inc. (CRM) (CRM:CA). The stock slipped to a new 52-week low this week. It's now down 29% year-to-date, wiping out half of its market cap since the 2025 high.

CRM previously suffered from a 58% peak-to-trough drawdown back in 2022, similar to the current one. If history repeats, we are close to the bottom.

This is a sentiment-driven market. Companies like Anthropic and other hyperscalers are fueling AI fears and creating volatility in the software sector. Whether or not AI can be disruptive remains to be seen, but any negative headline news can drive indiscriminate selling. I say this market mispricing creates bargains for investors who can identify long-term winners in the current agentic AI transition.

I believe CRM is well-positioned to outshine others, and its agentic AI platform has reached a major inflection. We can clearly see that the demand is increasing, with Agentforce and Data 365 contributing to higher revenue growth.

There are many other bullish signs. From the long history of achieving 40 Rule (FY2027 guidance implies 45%) to double-digit CAGR from FY26 to FY30, CRM will further expand agentic market shares over the next 5 years.

Three months ago, I upgraded the stock to Strong Buy after 3Q earnings. I'm surprised to see Agentforce adoptions were up nearly 50% sequentially, with 330% YoY ARR growth. Although large-scale AI monetization takes time to materialize, I saw an early revenue inflection at that time.

Some critics argue that software stocks should structurally trade at cheap valuations after the recent AI boom. At the sector level, it could happen, but it's not necessary to apply to every individual software stock. CRM's 14x non-GAAP P/E TTM is well below the S&P 500 utility and consumer staples sectors. I know it's not a direct comparison, but its P/E is even cheaper than the Chinese internet sector. Therefore, I think the stock is due for a rebound, and my Strong Buy still holds.

Agentforce 360 Ecosystem Is a Major Growth Driver

As I highlighted above, even with a sequential slowdown in Slack revenue in 4Q FY2026, "Agentforce 360 platform, Slack, and Other" revenue jumped 37% YoY to $2.7 billion on a constant currency basis. The segment is growing rapidly and now represents one fourth of CRM's revenue.

Agentforce and Data 360 ARR (including Informatica) reached $2.9 billion in 4Q, up 200% YoY. We saw 60% of Agentforce and Data 360 bookings came from contract expansion from existing customers.

Based on the current trend, I expect CRM's revenue growth will at least not decelerate in the next two years. In fact, the guided FY2027 revenue outlook implies a second consecutive year of acceleration.

Data Edge Can Make a Difference

Investors dumped CRM simply because of fears that third-party AI-generated code and agentic AI services will kill traditional software companies and permanently lower the sector's growth outlook.

In my recent article, I highlighted companies that can differentiate through their own data moats and adapt quickly to agentic AI will eventually survive.

So far, I don't think Anthropic's (ANTHRO) Claude will pose a major threat to CRM's Data 360, which is the foundational data layer for Agentforce. Regardless of which data repositories you are using, you can integrate them into Data 360 to harmonize your data. This creates higher switching costs for existing customers.

Moreover, CRM pivoted to agentic AI back in 2024, which gave them an "early mover" advantage in retaining customers and making a smooth transition to its own agentic AI platform.

Bookings and Backlogs Indicate Higher Demand for Agentic AI

CRM is an ultimate beneficiary of the agentic AI era, because the company's net new annual order value growth has been accelerating since FY2024 (the year of pivoting to agentic AI) and reached a positive 10% YoY growth in FY2026, driven by higher contract values and lower attrition rates.

The underlying demand can also be reflected by bookings and remaining performance obligations. Remaining performance obligations, or RPO, are the total amount of backlogs that will be recognized as future revenue. Current RPO (cRPO) is the value of the contract that will be realized within the next 12 months. For CRM, cRPO represents nearly 50% of its total RPO in 4Q FY2026.

We can see cRPO accelerated to 16.2% YoY in 4Q. Although they didn't provide booking numbers, we can calculate it by adding the sequential change in RPO balance to total revenue, which implies 23% YoY of current bookings growth in 4Q.

Based on 1Q FY2027 guidance, cRPO is expected to grow 14% YoY. However, this only implies a 5% YoY current bookings growth. However, this doesn't look too bad since the slowdown is driven by a high basis of cRPO balance from the previous quarter.

I believe the robust current bookings and cRPO will signal a strong revenue outlook in FY2027.

FY2027 Still Looks Very Solid

The upward trend in demand backlogs is expected to lift FY2027 revenue growth to 10.5% YoY, slightly up from 10% YoY in FY2026. The non-GAAP EPS would slow sharply to 5% next year due to a flat margin outlook. I think this is because the company will continue to invest heavily in agentic AI.

Its FCF would grow 9.5% YoY in FY2027, which translates to a 34.2% FCF margin, similar to 34.7% in FY2026. However, we should know that CRM's most investments are from M&A rather than capital expenditure, which can inflate free cash flow.

This is a High-Quality Company with a Cheap Valuation

CRM's 13x non-GAAP P/E is 43% below the sector median, showing a distressed valuation. Since the Seeking Alpha Charting page doesn't have non-GAAP multiples, I'll use the "cash flow from operations" multiple as a reference.

Its P/CF TTM is at 10.5x, down 60% from its 5-year average and currently at an all-time low. Compared to software leader Microsoft (MSFT), CRM is trading at a 40% discount.

To boost shareholder value, the company issued a $50 billion stock buyback and raised its quarterly dividend by 5.8% YoY in 4Q FY2026. This strongly demonstrates the management's confidence in the stock's long-term value.

The Bottom Line

With that being said, I strongly believe that CRM's backlog growth, cash flow quality, and distressed valuation together deserve a Strong Buy. Despite SaaSpocalypse fears, the stock is extremely undervalued given its early success of agentic AI transition and robust demand for Agentforce and Data 360. The P/E trading deeply below utility and staple sectors doesn't make sense to me. We've already seen the software sector start to bounce back from the recent lows. As sentiment improves, this is now a good time to be more aggressive on CRM.

data.title: Risk Event, Not Regime Shift: Why Markets Are Showing Resilience
data.description:
Iran tensions have pushed both oil prices and market volatility higher, but stocks have clawed back every point lost during the conflict. Brad Simpson, Chief Wealth Strategist, TD Wealth maps three scenarios for energy prices and breaks down what the rapid sentiment rebound is really signaling.

Transcript

Greg Bonnell - Equities have recovered the ground they lost due to the Middle East conflict. But should investors be expecting a choppy ride ahead, or can markets expect to show us some more resiliency? There's a lot going on there. Brad Simpson, Chief Wealth Strategist, TD Wealth, always great to have you in the studio, my friend.

Brad Simpson - Great to be here.

Greg Bonnell - I was just sitting -- and from a layman's perspective, I was just watching the action of the past month. There's this trigger event. It starts in Washington in terms of some sort of policy. And it just reminded me of last year. There was a trigger event. And what happened a month later in April - policy, the market sold off, and the markets regained almost in short order. How should we be reading this market?

Brad Simpson - Actually, no, I think that's a really great tie-in, actually, between the two. If you look at Liberation Day, maybe one of the most astonishing things was that the surprise of what was being implemented and then the correction occurred. It's kind of like it was telegraphed.

And then you fast-forward to here, and you've got two aircraft carriers sitting outside of Iran. The Polymarkets had it at 68% that by the end of March, there was going to be some sort of either escalation of some sort into Iran by the United States and Israel. So I think the starting point of that was, unlike Liberation Day, I think we really looked at the market reaction to it. I would say the market has been remarkably resilient.

Forget that. Let's go to the lows of this market and say, you're -- if you wanted to find the perfect day of the trade from top to bottom on the S&P 500, you can go from the highest, highest ever to the low. It would be a 10% correction. You'd probably, if we're being serious and go -- it's around our 6% or 7% correction for that.

And I think -- and a little bit of when we look at that, I think that the difference is that-- number one is that as we were moving through the fall of 2025, and you and I had talked about this, and we've written about at length, and we put our money where our mouth was on this -- was saying that the US economy, the Canadian economy, the European economy, and then even going into emerging markets, were starting to improve and starting to get -- and the more you looked at it, you started to go that it's been -- particularly in the United States, you got this early- to mid-stage economy turn-up. And the market in the last fall started to broaden out and started to move away from those big tech names. We're talking ultimately, about the software correction.

And so what was happening is you've had a market correction where really, what was happening is money was moving out of the growth and momentum. And what it was moving in towards was the broader market and moving into -- and that move, I think, almost accounts for more of the correction that we saw. And it was just a readjustment. And between the market expecting it and what was going on inside of that market, I think that -- you can explain away a lot of that here. And then you got to go, okay, now that we've done that, where do we go from here?

Greg Bonnell - Let's talk about where we go from here because the news flow is so intense on a daily basis. When I can read a headline that says the market regains its losses of the conflict, and for a day, there's not a crazy headline that makes me worry about everything, I take a sigh of relief. And I want to pretend that everything's going to be great. But let's talk about it. Is everything going to be great?

Brad Simpson - No, I don't think everything's going to be great. And I thought we would answer this. And you know that I don't have the power of foresight. But I thought I would bring this along. And what we're looking at is a scenario analysis that will be coming out in our quarterly strategy at the end of the quarter. So -- again, about a week or so here.

But looking at this, the most important one I want us to look at is description. So let's call that time. And key driver, let's call that inflation, sticky inflation or stagflation. These are the major determinants. So this is looking at going, what's going to happen in -- if we have this blockade that leads to a quick resolution, that's the bull case. And I'd be a little -- and in both cases, put a -- let's call it 20% scenario here, rapid de-escalation. Oil starts panning out to $70 or $80 a barrel. This is going from the end -- the beginning of this year, January 31 to December 31, 2026.

You would see the S&P 500, a bull case, could be up 24%. For the year, TSX up 19%. Look at the base case then, call it 90 to 110. You look at S&P 16 -- up 11%. What you're looking at there is you have stickier inflation. So interest rates-- instead of declining, which you do in the bull case, you see them staying where they're at.

The bear case is that it -- this gets longer and prolonged. And this gets $120 barrel of oil. So growth starts to -- not only does growth slow, you start to look at not inflation, but you start to see stagflation. So you see slower growth and higher inflation. What you see there is you actually see interest rates move up because you're kind of in this shock. And then you start to see them go down after that. And that's where you see a correction of call it the S&P 500, 15% and 19% there.

At least it starts to frame out what are the things to really look for. Duration, right? Time. How long does this play out because as long as it plays out, it impacts the oil price. And as long as the oil price -- it impacts on -- right now, inflation isn't a problem. That $90 to $100, inflation gets a little stickier. And that base case is where we're at right now.

And so I am comfortable with saying that is -- but if this gets -- if this becomes something more longer term, which it has a real potential to. I'm not sticking my head in the sand on this one. But at least, I think I wanted for your viewers to kind of go let's frame it out in nowhere. And then we know how we're going to build -- make decisions. And I think that's a terrific start.

Greg Bonnell - Since we don't know exactly where we end up in that frame, is there a sense there that sentiment is getting tested and that, perhaps, people are getting a little cautious in the markets?

Brad Simpson - You would think. But the reality is, we see something quite different than that. And I brought something -- I brought a -- I don't know if you guys have it. But I brought a chart along that we can actually look at there. So let's look at this.

So this is tying into -- if you look in the middle of the chart, it says "tariff chaos." And what you see there is that the black line is the S&P 500. The green is the greed and fear. It's our greed and fear. It's a proprietary measure that we made up of hard and soft data, of looking at what sentiment is. And what you're seeing is during Liberation Day, into that, you're seeing this plunging in. And as it actually is plunging, you're actually seeing the -- that fear is increasing. And then you're starting to go up as it starts to reduce.

You saw that during Liberation Day from -- you can even call it starting "Aprily" 2025 to now. And what we're looking on the far right-hand side of this chart is we're looking at same thing. You're going into private credit concerns, which was driving sentiment lower and getting more and more and more difficult. And then you get the war in Iran, and sentiment got very ugly.

What you're seeing now is you saw both the S&P 500 start to spike up on the far right there, the black line, and the green line, sentiment has dramatically improved. And what I account for that is, I think, a couple pieces. One, that in -- let's call it the last five years, and you and I have spoken about this a couple times is retail. Everyday investors have gotten way more involved in public markets in the short term. And they move very quickly on going from one place to the other place.

The second part that I think we've seen that are going on in markets is that a lot of trading in markets are done on algorithms in the short term now. And so if you go -- and so you go -- the way algorithms often work, it goes, well, oh, there's an invasion.

To my scenario analysis, oil could go high and could go high for a really long period of time. Oh, there's peace talks. Oh, oil could be softer. Stagflation won't happen. Things will get better. And the algo changes its trading mechanism. And so the movement of your -- the movement of trading based on headlines, based on short-term activities that you're taking out data and saying, Okay, but extrapolating what's going to go forward, has way more dramatic impact now than it used to.

And then the last one was this is I think if you looked at -- back to Liberation Day, as that rally started, I would argue a lot -- as an allocator of capital, a lot of long-only equity managers stayed on the sidelines and said, hey, look at this, and this time -- boy, I can't get left behind on here. So I think you're seeing some of that rally into this market, too. So you get the combination of the day trading and algos and the pros--

Greg Bonnell - This is the last year, right?

Brad Simpson - Yeah, all for three varying degrees of difference on why. And that gives you a pretty picture of why I think you see this sentiment change that you've seen as rapidly as it's gone.

Michael A. Cirami, CFA

Performance Discussion

The portfolio trended higher for Q1, outperforming the J.P. Morgan EMB Hard Currency/Local Currency 50/50 Index for the period.

Source: J.P. Morgan. As of 31 Mar 2026. Past performance does not guarantee and is not a reliable indicator of future results.

Investing Environment

Elevated geopolitical tensions replaced tariffs as the risk du jour for global capital markets in Q1 2026. Major events that otherwise would have been focal points, such as the removal of Venezuelan President Nicolás Maduro or the growing strain between the US and other NATO partners, quickly became afterthoughts as conflict in the Middle East took center stage.

The US and Israel launched air strikes on Iran beginning on February 28, citing the threat of the regime's ballistic missile program and potential nuclear capabilities. Iran responded in kind, retaliating with attacks against not only Israel and US assets in the Middle East but also other countries in the region. Iran effectively closed the Strait of Hormuz, a key shipping lane through which approximately 20% of global oil supply transits, causing energy prices to jump significantly. These higher prices and their potential impact on global inflation drove market volatility for the period.

All segments of emerging markets (EM) debt experienced negative trending performance as a result of the fraught geopolitical environment. EM corporate bonds returned -0.21%, outperforming their hard currency and local currency counterparts. EM hard and local currency bonds returned -1.22% and -2.25%, respectively.

Sovereign and corporate credit spreads widened over the quarter, as investors began to price in higher risk amid the conflict with Iran. Sovereign credit spreads widened more than corporate credit spreads, while high yield sovereigns declined to a greater degree than their investment-grade sovereign peers over the quarter.

Venezuela was among the top-performing sovereign credits. Following the removal of President Maduro, market participants expressed optimism around sanctions relief, increased hydrocarbon export revenue, and normalized relations with the US and other global trading partners. These factors boosted recovery value assumptions and the potential for debt restructuring. Conversely, Mozambique was among the worst performing sovereign credits. Underperformance reflected the ongoing security risks in the Cabo Delgado region and continued uncertainty around the restart of key LNG projects. Given the country's reliance on future gas revenues, these delays have weighed on expectations for growth and external financing. Combined with a fragile fiscal position, these factors left Mozambique particularly exposed in a more volatile macroeconomic environment.

Strong demand for EM debt supported robust sovereign and corporate issuance in Q1, totaling $241.6 billion, a 4.9% increase year over year. Supply was heavily front-loaded as issuers took advantage of supportive market conditions. Investment-grade borrowers drove primary market activity; Mexico and Saudi Arabia were leading contributors to issuance during the period. Although high yield issuance was limited, Ecuador, Benin and Cameroon were among the largest issuers that accessed capital markets in Q1. Inflows into the asset class were positive for the quarter, totaling $17.4 billion, with a small net outflow of -$1.7 billion in March following the onset of the conflict with Iran.

The US dollar strengthened in Q1, reflecting both its safe-haven status and the US' position as a net energy exporter. While most EM currencies depreciated during the quarter, oil-exporting currencies, or those relatively insulated from energy shocks, proved resilient. The Brazilian real, Argentine peso and Kazakhstani tenge were among the strongest performers. In contrast, high-beta, oil-importing currencies, including the Indian rupee, South Korean won and Thai baht, were among the weakest.

In developed markets, central banks held policy rates steady in Q1 as they assessed the inflationary impacts of higher energy costs. The Federal Reserve, ECB, BOE and BOJ all remained on hold. Across EM, central banks generally eased policy rates or paused. Many of the rate cuts occurred before the conflict with Iran escalated, reflecting the inflationary risks associated with higher oil prices. Colombia was a notable outlier, raising rates twice during the quarter to mitigate the risk of a wage-price spiral following a significant increase in the minimum wage and an overall sluggish disinflation trend.

While EM debt remains highly sensitive to macroeconomic and geopolitical factors, local developments continued to drive idiosyncratic performance across countries. In Uganda, President Museveni consolidated his hold on power following the January election. The aftermath has been marked by intensifying repression of the opposition, drawing increased scrutiny from the US, EU and other multilateral partners. In Bangladesh, the Bangladesh Nationalist Party (BNP) secured a strong mandate in the February election, raising expectations for policy normalization and institutional reform. Negotiations between Ukraine and Russia continued but made little substantive progress. The conflict increasingly resembled a de facto freeze as global attention shifted toward the Middle East.

Portfolio Positioning

In our view, the portfolio has remained conservatively positioned given the heightened geopolitical uncertainty that characterized Q1. This investing environment, however, continues to offer unique and interesting investment opportunities that the team is seeking to take advantage of. The portfolio hedges US rates from dollar-denominated investments and, as a result, is underweight duration relative to the J.P. Morgan EMB Hard Currency/Local Currency 50/50 Index. Across emerging markets, the portfolio holds overweight rates positions across Eastern Europe and Latin America, while maintaining underweight positions in Asia, where policy rates are generally lower. The portfolio's rates positioning weighed on relative performance during the quarter. On the credit side, the portfolio is overweight to sovereign credit relative to the index, favoring high yield issuers with strong fundamentals and spread tightening potential, while maintaining underweight exposure to select investment-grade names where we believe valuations are less compelling. Credit positioning has primarily focused on the front end of the curves, which overall has resulted in approximately one year underweight spread duration versus the index. Currency exposure is underweight relative to the benchmark; however, the portfolio maintains overweight positions in countries with strong macro fundamentals and attractive carry—primarily in Central Asia and Africa—while underweight those with elevated fiscal risk or where energy importation needs present downside risks, particularly in Asia.

The EMSights Capital Group continues to search for countries with improving storylines where the market prices are not fully reflecting fundamentals. The team continues to seek out idiosyncratic events in the sovereign and corporate space that can shape the market landscape and drive divergence between regions and countries, while maintaining a cautious stance on emerging markets debt. The global economy continues to face uncertainty plagued with elevated geopolitical tensions that we believe can present exploitable volatility in emerging markets debt.

data.title: Carlisle Companies: Roof Demand Drives Growth
data.description:
Co-authored by Kody's Dividends

Can you think of some of the basic needs for humans?

For some, the list of basic needs for humanity is that of food, water, and shelter: something to eat, something to drink, and somewhere to stay safe from the elements.

Over the years, humanity has built all sorts of different shelters, whether that is hiding in a cave or building various sizes of complex shelters in the form of buildings. Rarely do we think of the roof of a structure, except at the times that we build it or if we run into issues with it. Yet your roof protects the entirety of the rest of your structure. With no roof, rain would simply come in. A damaged roof will do no good against rain as well, because water finds every crack and crevice to work its way into. Roofs are not perpetual objects. They will need care or even replacement as time goes on to allow the rest of the building to continue to stand as needed.

Today, I want to revisit a company that we've covered before that focuses on the roofing needs of structures and how it is benefiting from the cyclical nature of demand for roofs across America as they age.

Let's dive in!

A Future Dividend King With A Trifecta Of Tailwinds

In 2025, Carlisle Companies (CSL) reported $5.02 billion in total revenue. Roughly three-quarters (~74%) of its revenue was derived from its Carlisle Construction Materials segment (the very profitable commercial re-roofing business). The remaining revenue came from CSL’s Weatherproofing Technologies segment (energy-efficient building envelopes for commercial and residential end-markets).

CSL’s management team had the confidence to reaffirm its commitment to delivering $40 in adjusted EPS by 2030. From our perspective, the company still has what it takes to, at the very least, come close to reaching this objective.

CSL has a few catalysts that should keep working in its favor over the next several years.

The average age of existing roofs on commercial buildings in the U.S. is above 25 years old. That means there will be a steady stream of demand for high-margin re-roofing demand for CSL through at least the end of the decade.

Not to mention that non-residential buildings today must comply with stricter environmental standards than in the past. Meeting these new energy standards will require more inches of insulation and higher-quality membranes per square foot than buildings 10 or 20 years ago. This is another revenue opportunity for CSL now and further down the road.

Finally, the massive labor shortage of skilled tradespeople has resulted in a pivot in CSL’s R&D focus on labor-saving products. The company’s 16-foot-wide EPDM sheets and self-adhering membranes are allowing smaller crews to dry-in a building in half the time compared to traditional methods. When contractors are short-staffed, they prioritize the products that let them finish jobs faster without sacrificing quality. That naturally benefits CSL.

The company also enjoys a BBB S&P credit rating with a stable outlook. This is because of its net debt to EBITDA ratio of 1.4x (in range of its 1x to 2x target) and $2.1 billion in liquidity, which gives it the flexibility to execute opportunistic bolt-on acquisitions.

That’s why the FAST Graphs analyst consensus is for CSL’s adjusted EPS to grow 6.7% in 2026 to $20.70 (a stabilization year). In 2027, adjusted EPS is expected to rise by 13.5% to $23.50. For 2028, another 15.7% jump in adjusted EPS to $27.20 is the current consensus.

CSL’s 1.3% dividend yield has plenty of room for future growth. That’s because CSL’s adjusted EPS payout ratio will likely register in the low-20% range for 2026. This is why we’re confident that the company can lengthen its 48-year dividend growth streak with 10%+ annual dividend growth on the horizon (for a Chowder number of at least 11).

From the current $357 share price (April 15, 2026), shares are trading at a forward 12-month PE ratio of 17.3. Put into perspective, that’s still much cheaper than its 10-year average PE ratio of 19.7, per FAST Graphs.

We think that a rational fair value multiple in the years ahead for CSL is around 18.5. The company’s 12.4% forward annual adjusted EPS growth consensus wouldn’t be too far off the 10-year average CAGR of 14.9. This demonstrates that CSL’s growth profile is holding up reasonably well in what’s currently a challenging environment for the company. That points to the stock being priced at a 16% discount to fair value.

If CSL returns to fair value and lives up to growth projections, it could deliver an 11% total return by the end of March 2027. Through 2031, CSL could produce 15% annual total returns.

Pertinent risks to the building envelope company include the challenging commercial new construction environment and rising input costs for a major product derivative.

While re-roofing provides a stable floor for CSL’s revenue, the high-interest-rate environment is having implications for commercial new construction. On the one hand, there’s the massive surge in data center construction that’s acting as a plus for nonresidential starts. On the other hand, traditional office starts are depressed due to overcapacity and high financing costs. The longer that these elevated rates persist, the more time it will take CSL to hit its Vision 2030 top-line goals.

Another risk that’s become important to point out in recent weeks is that a portion of the company’s building envelope products are petroleum-derived (e.g., synthetic rubber and thermoplastic membranes). In the face of rapidly rising WTI crude oil prices, CSL faces the prospect of margin compression if it can’t adjust product pricing fast enough to keep up with these growing costs.

Conclusion

CSL is a very cyclical company in many regards because it is exposed to the demand for roof repair, roof replacement, or new roofs across the United States. Its earnings can vary heavily. This can cause some investors to chase it upwards when earnings are growing beyond where it's worth, or to sell it off too heavily as earnings decline in its cyclical nature. The desire, though, is to see that cyclical nature provide higher highs and higher lows than previously.

Looking over its history, CSL has done a good job in growing its earnings through every cycle, and at this time, it is currently undervalued, making it an exciting opportunity as EPS is expected to grow once more.

Between our last coverage and this coverage, we saw a spike in value that caused it to go over what we think it was worth before declining back down into the buy range again. This is why it's always good to make sure that you know what price you think the company is worth buying for, and not chase it upwards just because it's climbing. A key aspect of our Dividend Kings model portfolio and our Dividend Kings analysis perspective is that we are driven by data and facts. We don't use FOMO, sentiment, or momentum to drive where we think something is worth. We use fundamental health. Currently, CSL is an outstanding opportunity for the future to provide strong returns to your portfolio. We rate shares a "Buy."

Know what you're buying and where it's going. Knowledge will provide you with significant emotional regulation and perspective. Something you can only get when you take a step away from the chaos and discover clarity.

data.title: Behind The Charts: Is The Energy Trade Running Out Of Fuel?
data.description:
By Christopher Gannatti, CFA

Recorded live on April 10, 2026, this Behind the Markets episode—“Behind the Charts”—features Macro Charts (MC) unpacking a market defined by shifting leadership, extreme positioning, and a critical inflection in energy. What follows is a distilled view of his framework and highest-conviction signals.

Hearing from a Disciplined Trend-Follower

MC’s core message was that he approaches markets as a disciplined trend follower, not as a macroeconomic forecaster. He repeatedly emphasized that his job is to listen to price action, identify dislocations between what people believe and what markets are actually doing, and then allocate capital with a tight focus on risk control. MC is not trying to predict every twist of the news cycle. Instead, he looks for the biggest trends, the moments when a move is becoming crowded or exhausted, and the places where risk-reward has become potentially asymmetric. That framework explains why he keeps revisiting sectors and themes as conditions evolve rather than locking into one permanent view.

The 'Energy Trade' has Done its Job

A major theme of the conversation was energy. MC made clear that he thinks the ‘energy trade’ has largely done its job. He described being heavily involved in the move higher but now believes the best part of the upside may be behind us. His view is not that energy becomes irrelevant from this point, but that the market is now closer to a topping phase than a fresh beginning.

MC supplied us with the following three figures to share, which give a further look at how he has shaped his thinking on the aforementioned ‘energy trade.’

Figure 1 highlights how extended the S&P Energy sector has become relative to its long-term trend, using the percentage distance above the 200-day moving average as a proxy for momentum and crowding. Historically, when this measure spikes to elevated levels, as seen in prior cycles around the mid-2000s and post-2020, energy has already experienced a significant run, often entering a late-stage phase rather than the beginning of a new move. The current reading shows a similarly stretched condition, suggesting that the recent rally has been both strong and potentially crowded. While this does not imply an immediate reversal, it does signal that forward returns may become more volatile and dependent on consolidation or a reset in positioning. In a broader portfolio context, such extremes often coincide with peak narrative strength and may precede rotation into lagging sectors as the energy trade matures.

Figure 1: Energy at Extremes: S&P Energy Sector Stretches Far Above Long-Term Trend

Figure 2 pairs the long-term trajectory of the S&P Energy sector with its 3-month rate of change, offering a clear view of momentum cycles within the broader trend. Periods highlighted in the chart show that when short-term gains spike to extreme levels, they tend to occur after a sustained move higher—not at the beginning of a rally. The current surge in 3-month returns stands out as one of the strongest in decades, aligning with prior episodes where energy experienced rapid, late-stage acceleration. Historically, these bursts of momentum often coincide with peak enthusiasm and crowded positioning rather than durable entry points. While strong momentum can persist longer than expected, it typically signals that the easy gains have already been realized. From a tactical perspective, this kind of setup suggests increasing sensitivity to negative surprises and a higher likelihood of consolidation, volatility, or rotation as the energy trade matures.

Figure 2: Energy Momentum Surge: 3-Month Gains Signal Late-Cycle Acceleration

This chart shows the S&P Energy sector relative to the broader S&P 500, paired with its 3-month rate of change, highlighting both trend leadership and momentum extremes. The key signal is the recent surge in short-term performance, which has reached a new all-time high in 3-month change. Historically, such spikes in relative momentum have occurred during sharp catch-up rallies or late-stage bursts of outperformance, rather than at the beginning of sustained leadership cycles. While energy has clearly regained relative strength after a prolonged period of underperformance, the magnitude and speed of the recent move suggest increasingly crowded positioning. These conditions often coincide with peak narrative conviction and can precede periods of consolidation or mean reversion. In this context, the chart reinforces the idea that while energy leadership has been powerful, the asymmetry may now be shifting as relative momentum reaches extreme levels.

Figure 3: Energy vs. Market: Record Momentum Signals Peak Relative Strength

Looking through Energy to the Oil Price

MC also sees the possibility of a broader normalization process in the price of oil but thinks that process will be messy and slow rather than clean and immediate. Because energy prices affect almost everything else, he framed oil as the central variable for the rest of the year. If oil rolls over or stabilizes, that could ripple through bonds, the consumer discretionary sector, international equities, and even inflation expectations.

That leads to one of his most interesting cross-asset ideas: if oil is peaking, then some of the market’s other beaten-up areas may finally have room to work. He was constructive on the consumer discretionary sector, arguing that it had been sold off aggressively and could benefit from a normalization in oil prices. He also said bonds can work in the same environment, which is a useful reminder that markets do not always behave in the simplistic “risk-on versus risk-off” way people expect. In his view, a combination of underinvestment, systematic buying, and positioning flushes may already have created a floor beneath the market, even if volatility remains high.

Broader Views on Equities

On the equity side, MC was careful to distinguish between what has already run and what still looks underappreciated. He suggested that parts of software and Software-as-a-Service (SaaS) may still have a second act, especially if the market broadens beyond the early leaders. He also liked Meta Platforms and Alphabet, not because he thinks they need to become generic AI winners, but because their AI capabilities can improve their core advertising businesses. In other words, he sees AI not as a standalone narrative, but as a tool that deepens the competitive moat of the best ad platforms. He made a similar point about valuation: big-tech multiples have compressed enough that these stocks are no longer obviously stretched in terms of their valuations relative to the broader market.

Going Beyond U.S. Borders

Internationally, MC pointed to the importance of oil for emerging markets and Asian economies, especially Japan, Korea, and China. His view was that a normalization in oil could provide meaningful relief in those regions, but investors may be too early if they try to buy only after the news is clearly good. The market, in his telling, usually moves before the story feels safe. He also thought that the current environment could be difficult for bears during earnings season, because positioning looks washed out, sentiment is poor, and many investors are underexposed. Overall, his message was pragmatic rather than heroic: be flexible, respect trends, focus on price, and look for the next pocket of opportunity as the current one matures.

Conclusion: Closer to Opportunity than Peak Risk

This chart tracks flows into money market and cash-like ETFs alongside the S&P 500, offering a window into investor positioning during periods of stress. Historically, sharp spikes in cash allocations have coincided with moments of heightened uncertainty, risk aversion, and, importantly, market inflection points. These surges often reflect a broad “de-risking” impulse, in other words, investors stepping to the sidelines after volatility or drawdowns. The current spike stands out as one of the more pronounced in recent years, suggesting a meaningful level of underinvestment and defensive positioning. From a contrarian perspective, these episodes frequently align with market bottoms or bottoming processes, as selling pressure becomes exhausted and sidelined capital creates latent buying power. While not a precise timing tool, the signal reinforces a key takeaway: when fear drives large reallocations to cash, the market is often closer to opportunity than to peak risk.

Figure 4: Capitulation or Caution? Cash Flows Surge as Markets Search for a Floor

Christopher Gannatti, CFA, Global Head of Research

Christopher Gannatti began at WisdomTree as a Research Analyst in December 2010, working directly with Jeremy Schwartz, CFA®, Director of Research. In January of 2014, he was promoted to Associate Director of Research where he was responsible to lead different groups of analysts and strategists within the broader Research team at WisdomTree. In February of 2018, Christopher was promoted to Head of Research, Europe, where he was based out of WisdomTree’s London office and was responsible for the full WisdomTree research effort within the European market, as well as supporting the UCITs platform globally. In November 2021, Christopher was promoted to Global Head of Research, now responsible for numerous communications on investment strategy globally, particularly in the thematic equity space. Christopher came to WisdomTree from Lord Abbett, where he worked for four and a half years as a Regional Consultant. He received his MBA in Quantitative Finance, Accounting, and Economics from NYU’s Stern School of Business in 2010, and he received his bachelor’s degree from Colgate University in Economics in 2006. Christopher is a holder of the Chartered Financial Analyst Designation.
