data.title: Reframing ImmunityBio: The Long-Term Platform Opportunity Beyond Anktiva
data.description:
Thesis

ImmunityBio (IBRX) stock has seen a lot of volatility in recent times. The stock seems to react very positively to good news but equally as negatively to negative news, as was the case with the FDA issue on the Anktiva commercial. As for my upside case, I think they certainly have the potential to evolve from an early-stage single-product story here into a much more scaled oncology and cell therapy platform company. I think in the near term, we will see continued adoption of Anktiva, which should be supported by guideline inclusion and the upcoming label expansion into papillary NMIBC. This could potentially drive revenue growth from the current $100 million to the $200 million range, toward several hundred million dollars annually. And beyond that, the real long-term upside lies in the successful execution of its Leonardo platform, which I’ll explain.

This could essentially enable scalable, repeatable deployment of NK cell-based therapies across global hospital networks. So going forward, if we see ImmunityBio leverage partnerships and infrastructure to industrialise cell therapy delivery while also managing to achieve positive clinical and regulatory outcomes in the near term, I think the company has a very credible path toward becoming a multi-billion-dollar revenue oncology platform. This is the case in which the current $8 billion valuation becomes justified. In my previous coverage, I focused a lot on Anktiva and the potential near-term upside. I now think it is a good idea to also focus on ImmunityBio's long-term outlook and broader platform potential.

1Q26 review

We got the latest financial update from ImmunityBio on April 9th, and on initial viewing, we can see a period of pretty rapid commercial acceleration, mainly driven by Anktiva. In 1Q26, the company reported preliminary net product revenue of about $44.2 million, representing a hefty 168% year-over-year increase. Anktiva is also showing us a continued upward trajectory since the drug’s commercial launch. However, this growth is not only sequential, with it being up about 15% from 4Q25, but it would also reflect some pretty strong market adoption among U.S. urologists, which is good to see. Essentially, this means Anktiva is gaining traction as a treatment option in bladder cancer care, particularly for BCG-unresponsive non-muscle invasive bladder cancer (NMIBC). FY25 revenue was $113 million, up about 700% from the same period last year. Again, this goes to show how early-stage commercialisation has really started to transition into meaningful revenue generation.

Just to touch briefly on their revenue trajectory here, I think it’s quite open to interpretation. On one hand, the company still managed to deliver $44.2 million in 1Q26 revenue, up 168% year-over-year, which definitely supports the bull case that Anktiva is gaining some decent commercial traction even though it’s still pretty early in its launch. However, if we take this on a sequential basis, growth has somewhat moderated to about 15% from 4Q25, so $38.3 million to the current $44.2 million figure, compared to about 20% growth in the prior quarter. Now, I'm not suggesting a potential early plateau here within the current CIS NMIBC indication. But it’s just something to keep an eye on this year.

As for operations, ImmunityBio still seems to be pretty well-capitalised. They managed to end the quarter with roughly about $381 million in cash and marketable securities, which should give them a solid runway to fund ongoing clinical development. As for the regulatory front, Anktiva has now already achieved approvals, or we could say authorisations, across five jurisdictions covering about 33 countries. The pipeline is still very much maturing, and we have the pivotal QUILT-2.005 trial to look forward to, which is now fully enrolled with a supplemental BLA expected sometime this year. Elsewhere, I think the inclusion in the National Comprehensive Cancer Network clinical guidelines under Category 2A can’t be understated here. It definitely enhances the overall credibility and could help further accelerate adoption later on. So overall, the report tells me that the company is still transitioning from development to commercialisation success, with some decent revenue growth, expanding clinical validation, and sufficient liquidity to sustain the same undertaking going forward.

Global expansion narrative still on

Now, with ImmunityBio, there has been quite a lot of support for the bull case, even among all the short interest. For me, my upside case is definitely centred around how ImmunityBio is shifting beyond its currently approved product, Anktiva, towards somewhat of a global platform strategy. So going forward, I can certainly picture a scenario in which ImmunityBio enters into a number of collaborations with existing clinics within healthcare networks with major international hospitals in the locations they're looking to expand in, like Abu Dhabi and the UK. The strategic logic here is that instead of building that costly infrastructure from scratch, ImmunityBio could rather leverage existing clinics’ footprint, patient access, and regulatory familiarity across those regions. The goal here would, of course, be to accelerate international deployment of both their therapies and manufacturing capabilities. I think this becomes particularly relevant for their advanced immunotherapies. We usually see that distribution in this area depends a lot on integrating into hospital systems capable of handling the complex biologics and cell-based treatments.

This outlook would then tie directly into my second upside-down case. We know that ImmunityBio is evolving from a single-drug biotech into a vertically integrated cell therapy platform company, which we like to see. But what’s a lot more essential to gaining momentum here would be their Leonardo platform. Keep in mind, this is essentially a closed-loop cell therapy manufacturing system. Think of it as an infrastructure that can extract immune cells via apheresis and then expand and activate them using cytokine-based agents like Anktiva. This is what’s then reinfused into patients at scale. So this platform is indeed a proprietary platform, and that distinction is going to be important because it reframes the way you should look at the company. You see, Leonardo is designed as an integrated cell therapy manufacturing/delivery system, so it's not just a concept or partnership-driven idea. The goal here would then be to create a standardised, scalable process for immune cell therapies. This is something that we’ve seen be a major bottleneck in biotech in the past. So in having this platform, the company can perform those steps like apheresis, where they extract immune cells from patients/donors, expanding and activating those cells ex vivo, and then reinfusing them back into patients. What I feel makes Leonardo strategic is that it aims to industrialise this whole process, in that it has the potential to turn what would typically be a complex, bespoke therapy into something closer to a repeatable system. I see this as a very front-and-centre long-term aim for ImmunityBio.

Having said that, this is where the scenario of clinic partnerships becomes rather important. If ImmunityBio wants to scale Leonardo globally, it would, of course, need physical clinical and manufacturing nodes embedded within a number of hospital systems. So, places that already have patient flow, regulatory approvals, and clinical infrastructure. Rather than exporting a therapy here, they would basically be deploying their platform into hospitals, using those networks as distribution/treatment hubs for multiple therapies like M-ceNK, haNK, and iNKT cells. That’s why I see so much upside from partnerships in regions like the UAE or Europe. These are markets that often have centralised healthcare systems and large flagship hospitals. I feel they would be ideal for rolling out platform-based therapies.

So yes, Anktiva is the first commercial product and biological backbone that gives us IL-15 activation, but Leonardo can’t be overlooked for investors holding ImmunityBio long term. If it works, ImmunityBio then has the potential to transition from a company selling a single oncology drug to one that controls both the therapy and the manufacturing ecosystem behind that next-generation immunotherapy. This makes the long-term bull case; it’s a much larger, but also much more execution-dependent, opportunity.

Regulatory tension and risk

Having just mentioned my bull case, I think it’s best to now focus on the current reality. So, on that topic, we have to mention the current tension with the FDA. It’s contributed to a hefty pullback in share price and needs to be placed in the context of the upcoming regulatory milestones. As you know, it relates to the company’s promotional practices, specifically around a direct-to-consumer television advertisement for Anktiva. The FDA wasn’t too fond of the ad, to say the least, and they ended up raising some concerns about the ad for potentially omitting/underemphasizing key risk information and presenting efficacy in a way that could somewhat be considered misleading. And yes, lots of investors claim the ad was never widely aired and would argue that this was a procedural or even technical issue. Particularly since the company leadership publicly reiterated safety disclosures afterwards, but I think the situation here still highlights a much deeper concern. It’s clear that ImmunityBio is willing to push aggressive messaging early in its commercial lifecycle, and some may see that as a positive, but the reality is that it’s attracted some regulatory scrutiny.

Approvals, label expansions, and trial pathways are all tightly controlled by the FDA; there’s no changing that. In fact, even minor compliance issues can put a lot of strain on relationships with regulators, and keep in mind both parties will have discussions and meetings that are intended to support drug development. So, for me, the implication is not just reputational; it does introduce a lot of execution risk. One case would be that FDA oversight could slow down future communications or complicate labelling discussions. Or, in a worst-case scenario, influence the tone of ongoing regulatory interactions, which we wouldn't want. And for a company whose near-term valuation depends pretty heavily on future approvals and rapid expansion, maintaining that smooth regulatory relationship is going to be key. So that makes this episode a somewhat non-trivial risk factor, even though there seems to be limited immediate impact.

Now, at the same time, investor focus is going to be pretty centred on a key near-term regulatory catalyst: the sBLA for Anktiva in BCG-unresponsive NMIBC with papillary-only disease. I went into a lot of detail on this catalyst in my previous coverage, but as for now, the filing was submitted around March 9th this year and would then start with the FDA’s standard 60-day review window to decide whether to accept the application for filing. As you know, this acceptance decision is crucial. It’s set to signal that the submission is sufficiently complete for formal review. And, if accepted, the FDA would then assign a PDUFA target date for us to look forward to. It would typically be about 6 months later, which would set up a massive catalyst later this year. Keep in mind that this pathway would represent a very meaningful commercial expansion beyond the current CIS indication, which would therefore make it central to the near-term growth story.

However, the risk is a bit asymmetric in that any delay in the FDA’s acceptance decision beyond the expected window, or worse, a refusal-to-file, would very much be seen as a negative signal, particularly regarding the strength/completeness of the data package. As you know, the pull-backs in share price have been quite harsh in recent times, and it would seem the market is reacting very strongly to bad news with ImmunityBio stock. If we take the earlier regulatory friction around promotional practices, investors, I think, would then be particularly sensitive to any signs of FDA hesitation. It makes this upcoming milestone one of the most important near-term inflection points for the stock.

Valuation

I think the current valuation profile of ImmunityBio, despite the pullback in share price from that mid-$11 range, is still pretty reflective of a market pricing in aggressive future growth here. Their trailing twelve-month revenue is still quite modest, and the company trades at some very elevated EV to sales multiples of 76x trailing and 41x forward. These are far above the sector median, which is closer to the 3x to 4x range, which tells me investors are assigning a substantial premium for the anticipated expansion of Anktiva. Elsewhere, the traditional profitability metrics are still quite non-meaningful, since the company is still very much in a pre-earnings or early commercialisation phase.

They hold an enterprise value of about $8.6 billion, and I see a market that is effectively pricing in a scenario where ImmunityBio transitions from its current $100 million to $200 million revenue scale to a multi-billion-dollar oncology platform over time. For this, we would need successful clinical readouts, regulatory expansions, and also sustained adoption, driving operating leverage. So, in short, the stock’s valuation here would imply high confidence in long-term execution and also blockbuster potential, but this leaves very little room for setbacks in clinical/regulatory or commercial progress.

Looking ahead

As for the balance sheet, I think they’re still in a pretty good place. We saw some recent financing that actually tried to minimise shareholder dilution. The company managed to secure about $75 million in non-dilutive capital from Oberland Capital. So now the total funding under the royalty agreement is about $375 million, which should give them immediate liquidity to support the commercialisation of Anktiva. Now, at the same time, we can see that an affiliated entity of Patrick Soon-Shiong converted about $25 million of debt into equity, which is about 4.6 million shares, so that it modestly reduced outstanding liabilities. But keep in mind this is tied to a much larger $505 million promissory note. With that royalty structure, it means ImmunityBio will probably pay a higher percentage of future sales, possibly 5.6% to 12.5%, but there's a key near-term benefit in that the company gets to extend its cash runway without tapping public markets for a dilutive equity raise here.

Overall, here, my thesis on ImmunityBio is that we can clearly see signs of the company transitioning from an early commercial-stage biotech anchored by Anktiva into more of a broader, long-term oncology/cell therapy platform story. In the near term, continued adoption of Anktiva is what investors will most likely be focused on. We have support from guideline inclusion, international expansion, and upcoming label extensions, like the papillary NMIBC. This should drive revenue steadily higher. However, I also feel the real upside lies beyond the current product cycle and more so in the successful execution of their Leonardo platform. For me, this has the potential to industrialise NK-cell-based therapies. If we do see ImmunityBio successfully navigate the current regulatory risks and maintain good clinical momentum, then the company could evolve into a multi-billion-dollar oncology platform in an upside case scenario.

data.title: Dole: Strategic Portfolio Shift Strengthens This Real Food Leader's Re-Rating
data.description:
Introduction

Back when I first covered Dole plc (DOLE), I called them "A Deeply Undervalued Way To Invest In Real Food," highlighting their attractive valuation and exposure to "real food" that can benefit from long-term tailwinds.

Following a solid year marked by important fundamental shifts towards building a higher-margin and value-added portfolio, Dole remains a Strong Buy, backed by strong financials and potential to benefit from consumer trends while being a defensive business.

Fundamental Shift Continues

Dole reported mixed Q4 results, with a slight miss on EPS despite a beat on revenue, although the Adj. EBITDA reached $72.7 million, which was above the market's expectations. The free cash flow reached only $1.71 million in 2025 ($67.9 million in Q4) compared to $180.29 million in 2024 and $220.56 million in 2023 as a result of a mix between much higher CAPEX ($121.5 million in 2025 vs. $82.44 million and $78.04 million, respectively) - which included the buyout of 2 vessel finance leases for $36.1 million last year - plus a significant working capital hit and a fall in commodity prices.

2025 came with several advancements in terms of portfolio adjustments, with the sale of their low-margin Fresh Vegetables division for ~$140 million, agreement to sell port assets in Ecuador for net proceeds of ~$75 million, $1.2 billion renewal of their credit facilities, among other developments such as transitioning to full U.S. Domestic Issuer filings and potentially attracting more attention from the market or even potential index inclusions.

For 2026, Dole expects at least $400 million in EBITDA, with routine CAPEX of ~$100 million and ~$60 million worth of interest expenses compared to $66.54 million in 2025 and $72.26 million in 2024, guiding again for a long-term 30% to 35% FCF conversion rate based on their Q4 Earnings Call:

Generally, we've said free cash flow conversion of between 30% and 35% over the longer term. We have outperformed that, Chris, you're quite right, over the last few years. But yes, I mean, we're targeting more normalized levels, maybe not as strong as we saw last year with a particularly strong inflow at the end of last year. So that makes the comparison a little bit more challenging. But 30% to 35% is the number we generally recommend people to consider for the longer term.

Financially, based on Dole's latest report, we can still see a solid position, with current assets covering their current liabilities and a good chunk of their manageable long-term debt, reducing their net debt by $30.7 million in 2025 and getting to a net leverage of 1.5x.

Alongside their 2.28% dividend that represented $31.57 million in 2025, Dole announced a $100 million buybacks program in November, with no set expiration date. As of filing their Q4 report, they bought $4.5 million YTD, although we are likely to see them advance on this during their next report.

Dole's next earnings report is scheduled for May 12, with the market expecting $0.34 in Normalized EPS and $2.23 billion in revenue. Although I don't expect major changes in their guidance or operations, I'll pay very close attention to their insights into the industry, as there are several macro factors to watch when it comes to their key crops, from the boom in fertilizer prices due to the Iran conflict, biological threats advancing, as well as potential for more consumer weakness.

The Environment Is Getting Even Tougher

Right now, the Iran conflict will probably be the most important factor that dictates the company's near-term performance, with new warnings of a recession coming from the IMF in case the conflict doesn't de-escalate quickly.

Even if it does however, it's important to remember that this region is crucial for fertilizers as well on top of oil and gas, being responsible for nearly one third of global urea trade and over 40% of the world's sulfur, with the deputy executive director of the World Food Program recently highlighting its effects on the current planting season, mentioning that we either see lower yields and crop failures next season in the worst case, or higher input costs next year that will ultimately be passed to the consumer in "the best case."

Alongside higher fuel prices, this can result in a significant wave of inflation that the Fed and other central banks will have to address, potentially hurting discretionary budgets even more through higher borrowing costs besides the fact that most companies will likely pass these extra costs to the final consumer.

In recent months, we can see the price of bananas continue their rise, as Tropical Race 4 is expanding and has been confirmed in Ecuador in late 2025, alongside the spread of the Moko bacterial disease threatening the global banana supply, with the country's banana production projected to drop by 4% to 5% in 2025-2026 due to the latter combined with the lack of optimum sunlight (that's before TR4).

There are some developments coming from the Taiwan Banana Research Institute, which developed TR4-resistant Cavendish variants (recently achieving up to 70% to 90% resistance) of the banana to make it more resilient to this, but that can still take significant efforts and money to adopt, while Fresh Del Monte's (FDP) CEO called this a losing battle not long ago, as highlighted during their previous coverage, while other regions could emerge as new banana powerhouses in the future. This can also result in another boom in banana prices however, as weaker supply combined with higher shipping and fertilizer costs can certainly add up.

However, I believe this also makes it even more important to have exposure to real food, as it can prove to be more resilient during times of economic weakness than most other stocks traditionally considered defensive. Also, fruits and vegetables are one of the very few products that aren't threatened by the retailers' private labels, a trend that poses very significant long-term fundamental risks for players like General Mills (GIS), Conagra Brands (CAG) or Campbell's (CPB) now have to fight with titans like Walmart (WMT) for the weaker consumer's budget marked by trade-downs.

This specific segment can prove to be much more resilient than what the market has traditionally considered defensive stocks, with Deutsche Bank recently sharing a warning for the consumer-packaged goods landscape, effectively questioning the defensiveness of these legacy brands in the face of trends and private labels.

Not only that, but given the ongoing consumer trends and potential improvements in health-awareness over the long-term, it's not impossible to see some boosts in demand, especially if GLP-1 medicine, MAHA, and other trends make people move away from snacks or sweets into healthier alternatives. This can allow the company to continue expanding their value-added portfolio, which can turn them into more than a simple commodity business, similar to how Cal-Maine Foods (CALM) did with eggs following their significant investments in specialty eggs and prepared foods, as mentioned in the latest coverage.

Risks Reiterated

Although Dole is advancing towards more than a simple commodity company, the specific issues in the banana segment remain significant, potentially leading to even more volatility in the near-term despite their long-term potential - even though this volatility can go both ways.

At the same time, the Iran conflict can rapidly get worse, potentially leading to even more pressure on the consumer and higher borrowing costs for the company and its partners, which is far from a great timing given the biological threat the industry has to deal with.

Valuation

For Dole's DCF to Equity valuation, based on the midpoint of their long-term FCF conversion rate guidance (32.5%) and the "at least $400 million EBITDA" guidance, I'll assume a base free cash flow of $130 million in 2026, with no changes in net borrowings given their solid balance sheet.

Thanks to their expansion into higher value-added products that can improve margins, long-term consumer trends, and potential from buybacks to boost the final per share result, I'll assume a 5% CAGR over the first 5 years, followed by a 3.5% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years, with a 2.5% terminal growth rate and a 10% discount rate to assume a solid margin of safety and 96.1 million diluted shares outstanding as of their Q4 report.

This gets us to an equity value of $2.04 billion, for an intrinsic value of $21.18 per share, which is still above the current $14.85 at the moment of writing this.

Conclusion

Dole remains a Strong Buy, backed by a strong and improving business that's navigating the current environment well, offering exposure to real food and standing to benefit from long-term consumer trends.

There are risks to consider given the current environment and the potential inflationary shock that can affect the consumer even more, but the current price already seems to offer a solid margin of safety for a solid company to own for the long-term.

data.title: Baird Mid Cap Growth Equity Q1 2026 Commentary And Market Outlook
data.description:
Market Update

Equities rallied to start the year, spurred by falling interest rates along with an uptick in economic activity and related company fundamentals. However, as March began, macro conditions became much less forgiving. U.S. military action against Iran, followed by escalation across the Middle East, helped push oil prices above $100 per barrel, while firmer-than-expected inflation data added to concerns around the level of interest rates and the sustainability of economic growth. This unfavorable combination introduced a higher level of market volatility and ultimately drove broad equity returns into negative territory for the quarter.

Portfolio Commentary

The Baird Mid Cap Growth portfolio declined -2.2%, net of fees, in the quarter, outperforming the Russell Midcap Growth Index, which decreased -6.4%.* This outperformance was driven by strong relative results in the portfolio's two largest sectors, consumer discretionary and industrials, where new idea generation and positive capital allocation decisions enabled both sectors to outpace their respective benchmark sectors. In addition, the market backdrop for our investment style was more constructive than in recent quarters, with profitable, higher-return businesses outperforming. More detailed commentary on sectors and portfolio changes follows.

The industrials sector was the largest contributor to positive relative performance. Strength in holdings such as Curtiss-Wright (CW), BWX Technologies (BWXT), Woodward (WWD), EMCOR (EME), and Hubbell (HUBB) reflected the market's increasing appreciation for durable spending tied to aerospace and defense, electric grid investment, and infrastructure. The sector's best performing holding was trucking company XPO (XPO), as the industry's multi-year downturn appears to be ending, with improved volumes and strong pricing supporting a favorable growth outlook. UL Solutions (ULS) also performed well.

We were active during the quarter in this sector, adding four new positions, three of which were among the most significant contributors. Hubbell, designs, manufactures, and sells electrical products for construction, industrial, and utility applications and is positioned to benefit from a demand inflection as global electricity consumption increases, particularly from data centers and large infrastructure projects. Woodward is a leading supplier of mission-critical aircraft engine control systems, with significant aftermarket and defense exposure, as well as a presence in industrial motion-control markets. UL Solutions is a high-quality testing and certification business with resilient growth and self-help potential. Verisk is a competitively advantaged information business serving the insurance industry, where we believe recent weakness overstated AI-related risks. Offsetting these additions, we exited BWX Technologies following a strong run, as we believe similar end-market exposure can be achieved with more attractive risk/reward elsewhere. We also exited Zebra Technologies (ZBRA) due to inconsistent execution. Trex (TREX) was sold to reduce housing exposure in a more volatile rate environment, and we exited Shift4 Payments (FOUR) as our conviction in both the company-specific outlook and broader industry dynamics declined. The net effect of these changes was an increased allocation to a sector where we see several industries with strong and improving fundamentals.

The consumer discretionary sector was another key source of outperformance. Restaurant and experiential holdings such as CAVA (CAVA), Wingstop (WING), Hilton (HLT), and Live Nation (LYV) responded well to earnings results and improving 2026 growth expectations, while Kontoor Brands and BJ's Wholesale Club also contributed. Wingstop and Dutch Bros (BROS) declined on concerns about the potential impact of higher oil prices on consumers; however, we believe the long-term growth opportunity outweighs this risk. We continue to opportunistically increase our exposure to the sector, while remaining mindful of volatility stemming from the conflict with Iran. Companies such as Floor & Décor (FND) and Wingstop weakened alongside rising rates and gasoline prices, but we used that volatility to add selectively where our core theses remain intact. We added Delta Air Lines (DAL), reflecting our view that the U.S. airline industry is entering a more rational, supply-constrained phase, with Delta best positioned given its premium franchise, loyalty ecosystem, operational execution, and balance sheet. We also initiated a position in Victoria's Secret (VSCO), a leading specialty retailer of branded apparel and beauty products. Under new leadership, we see improved marketing and brand positioning, along with tailwinds from a broader resurgence in mall-based apparel, which should support stronger sales growth and margin expansion over time. We exited e.l.f. Beauty (ELF) as our confidence in the brand's growth and market share trajectory diminished.

Consumer staples, despite its modest overall weight, made a solid contribution. Casey's General Stores (CASY) remained a steady performer, supported by strong fuel margins and solid in-store execution. We initiated a position in Brown-Forman (BF.A), where a prolonged period of underperformance has created an attractive setup relative to modest expectations for this leading beverage provider.

Healthcare lagged the benchmark, largely due to a broad pullback in medtech, where in several cases, solid fundamental performance did not translate into good stock returns. Early in the quarter, Penumbra (PEN) contributed positively following its announced sale to Boston Scientific (BSX). We sold following the acquisition and initiated a position in Align Technology (ALGN), the market leader in dental clear aligners, where we see potential for improving domestic trends, continued international expansion, and margin upside, particularly with activist involvement. We also reinitiated IDEXX Laboratories (IDXX), a leading developer and manufacturer of proprietary diagnostic instruments, consumables, and software primarily serving animal veterinary clinics. We also added to long-time holdings Insulet (PODD) and DexCom (DXCM), as we believe price weakness created attractive entry points into businesses with durable, recurring growth characteristics. We look for improved performance from a sector that has served us well historically.

Performance within technology was mixed but modestly positive relative to the benchmark. Early in the quarter, we reduced exposure to software and services, exiting PTC (PTC) and EPAM Systems (EPAM). These sales reflect our evolving and updated view that AI-driven disruption may put these two business models at severe competitive risk. Some of this capital was reallocated to Veeva Systems (VEEV), where we believe its vertical-market positioning in healthcare is more defensible. We continue to reassess software models overall in the face of AI developments, be it the sustainability of what were historically durable, fast growing recurring revenue models, or the historically high margins and rich valuations. AI- and data center-related enthusiasm remained supportive for certain holdings, with Vertiv and Monolithic Power (MPWR) notable beneficiaries. We trimmed both positions following significant strength, although this proved premature in the near term given their continued resilience and performance through the March volatility.

Financials contributed positively to relative performance, supported by solid trends at MSCI (MSCI) and Raymond James (RJF), as well as our limited exposure to weaker areas of the sector, particularly alternative asset managers and certain insurance-related businesses that declined during the quarter. While we made limited changes in the sector, results continue to reinforce our preference for businesses with durable data, advisory, and market structure advantages.

Outside the core sector groupings, energy was a relative headwind, as our underweight to the sector detracted during the spike in oil prices, although Viper Energy (VNOM) performed well on an absolute basis. Materials exposure was more supportive, with Fastenal (FAST) benefiting from improving industrial activity

Outlook

We enter the second quarter with a macro backdrop that is clearly more complex than at the start of the year. Military conflict has put geopolitical risk at the fore, while higher oil prices introduced additional upside risk to already persistent inflation making the path of interest rates remain uncertain; not to mention the tremendous potential and uncertainty brought on by the progress of AI. Further bouts of volatility and significant stock price swings should be expected.

In our view, the first quarter served as a useful reminder that fundamentals matter. Businesses with strong returns on capital, resilient margins, differentiated assets, and exposure to durable investment cycles received greater recognition from the market. Our long-term investment approach is built on these characteristics.

The portfolio remains well diversified in differentiated mid-cap companies with attractive risk/reward characteristics. Our goal is to use periods of volatility opportunistically to enhance overall portfolio fundamentals and long-term return potential. We are encouraged by the start to the year and look forward to the work ahead. On behalf of the entire team at Baird Equity Asset Management, thank you for your continued support of our Mid Cap Growth Strategy.
